content:
# Leviticus Chapter 25 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 73r–73v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_025_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_025_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 111–112 | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_025_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai, saying:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land that I am giving you, the land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD.

**3** For six years you shall sow your field, and for six years you shall prune your vineyard and gather in its produce,

**4** but in the seventh year there shall be a Sabbath of solemn rest for the land, a Sabbath to the LORD. You shall not sow your field or prune your vineyard.

**5** What grows of itself from your harvest you shall not reap, and the grapes of your undressed vine you shall not gather. It shall be a year of solemn rest for the land.

**6** The Sabbath of the land shall be food for you: for you and for your male servant and for your female servant and for your hired worker and for the sojourner who lives with you,

**7** and for your cattle and for the wild animals that are in your land: all its yield shall be for food.

**8** "You shall count seven weeks of years for yourself, seven times seven years, so that the time of the seven weeks of years shall give you forty-nine years.

**9** Then you shall sound the loud trumpet on the tenth day of the seventh month. On the Day of Atonement you shall sound the trumpet throughout all your land.

**10** And you shall consecrate the fiftieth year, and proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants. It shall be a Jubilee for you, when each of you shall return to his property and each of you shall return to his clan.

**11** That fiftieth year shall be a Jubilee for you. In it you shall neither sow nor reap what grows of itself nor gather the grapes from the undressed vines.

**12** For it is a Jubilee. It shall be holy to you. You may eat the produce of the field.

**13** "In this year of Jubilee each of you shall return to his property.

**14** And if you make a sale to your neighbor or buy from your neighbor, you shall not wrong one another.

**15** You shall pay your neighbor according to the number of years after the Jubilee, and he shall sell to you according to the number of years for crops.

**16** If the years are many, you shall increase the price, and if the years are few, you shall reduce the price, for it is the number of harvests he is selling to you.

**17** You shall not wrong one another, but you shall fear your God, for I am the LORD your God.

**18** "Therefore you shall do my statutes and keep my rules and perform them, and then you will dwell in the land securely.

**19** The land will yield its fruit, and you will eat your fill and dwell in it securely.

**20** And if you say, 'What shall we eat in the seventh year, if we may not sow or gather in our crop?'

**21** I will command my blessing on you in the sixth year, so that it will produce a crop sufficient for three years.

**22** When you sow in the eighth year, you will be eating some of the old crop; you shall eat the old until the ninth year, when its crop arrives.

**23** "The land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine. For you are strangers and sojourners with me.

**24** And in all the country you possess, you shall allow a redemption of the land.

**25** "If your brother becomes poor and sells part of his property, then his nearest redeemer shall come and redeem what his brother has sold.

**26** If a man has no one to redeem it and then himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means to redeem it,

**27** let him calculate the years since his sale and pay back the balance to the man to whom he sold it, and then return to his property.

**28** But if he does not have sufficient means to recover it, then what he sold shall remain in the hand of the buyer until the year of Jubilee. In the Jubilee it shall be released, and he shall return to his property.

**29** "If a man sells a dwelling house in a walled city, he may redeem it within a full year after its sale. For a full year he shall have the right of redemption.

**30** If it is not redeemed within a full year, then the house in the walled city shall be made permanent property of the buyer throughout his generations; it shall not be released in the Jubilee.

**31** But houses in villages that have no surrounding wall shall be classified with the fields of the land. They may be redeemed, and they shall be released in the Jubilee.

**32** "As for the cities of the Levites, the houses in the cities they possess, the Levites may redeem at any time.

**33** And if one of the Levites exercises his right of redemption, then the house that was sold in a city they possess shall be released in the Jubilee. For the houses in the cities of the Levites are their possession among the sons of Israel.

**34** But the fields of pastureland belonging to their cities may not be sold, for that is their permanent possession.

**35** "If your brother becomes poor and cannot maintain himself with you, you shall support him as though he were a sojourner and a resident alien, and he shall live with you.

**36** Take no interest from him or profit, but fear your God, that your brother may live beside you.

**37** You shall not lend him your money at interest, nor give him your food for profit.

**38** I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan, and to be your God.

**39** "If your brother becomes poor beside you and sells himself to you, you shall not make him serve as a slave.

**40** He shall be with you as a hired worker and as a sojourner. He shall serve with you until the year of the Jubilee.

**41** Then he shall go out from you, he and his children with him, and go back to his own clan and return to the possession of his fathers.

**42** For they are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt; they shall not be sold as slaves.

**43** You shall not rule over him ruthlessly but shall fear your God.

**44** As for your male slave and your female slave whom you may have: you may buy male and female slaves from among the nations that are around you.

**45** You may also buy from among the sojourners who are temporary residents among you and from their families that are with you, who have been born in your land, and they may become your property.

**46** You may bequeath them to your sons after you to inherit as a possession forever. You may make slaves of them, but over your brothers the sons of Israel you shall not rule, one over another, ruthlessly.

**47** "If a sojourner or a resident alien with you becomes rich, and your brother beside him becomes poor and sells himself to the sojourner or resident alien with you or to a member of the sojourner's clan,

**48** then after he is sold he may be redeemed. One of his brothers may redeem him,

**49** or his uncle or his cousin may redeem him, or a near relative from his clan may redeem him. Or if he grows rich enough, he may redeem himself.

**50** He shall calculate with his buyer from the year when he sold himself to him until the year of Jubilee, and the price of his sale shall be based on the number of years. The time he was with his buyer shall be rated as the time of a hired worker.

**51** If there are still many years left, he shall pay proportionately for his redemption some of his sale price.

**52** If there remain but a few years until the year of Jubilee, he shall calculate and pay for his redemption in proportion to his years of service.

**53** He shall treat him as a worker hired year by year. He shall not rule over him ruthlessly in your sight.

**54** And if he is not redeemed by these means, then he and his children shall be released in the year of Jubilee.

**55** For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"On Mount Sinai" (v. 1):** Hebrew בְּהַר סִינַי. The specification of Mount Sinai as the location of this discourse is notable and unique within Leviticus. Elsewhere the Sinai setting is background; here it is foregrounded. This opening phrase ties the Jubilee legislation directly to the Mosaic covenant at Sinai, grounding land theology in the original divine grant. The LXX confirms ἐν τῷ ὄρει Σινα.

2. **"The land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD" (v. 2):** Hebrew וְשָׁבְתָה הָאָרֶץ שַׁבָּת לַיהוָה. The verb שָׁבַת ("to rest, to cease") with the land as subject is unusual and theologically significant: the land itself observes the Sabbath, as though it too is bound in covenant relationship with the LORD. The Sabbath is not merely Israel's but the land's. The LXX renders ἀναπαύσεται ἡ γῆ σάββατα τῷ κυρίῳ.

3. **"Sabbath of solemn rest" (vv. 4, 5):** Hebrew שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן, the intensive doubled form. LOGOS renders it "Sabbath of solemn rest" rather than "complete Sabbath rest" to preserve the elevation of the construction above the ordinary שַׁבָּת. The same phrase appears for the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:31) and the weekly Sabbath (Lev 23:3). The LXX σάββατα ἀνάπαυσις reflects the doubled intensity.

4. **"What grows of itself" (vv. 5, 11):** Hebrew סְפִיחַ, a technical agricultural term for grain that self-germinates from the previous year's fallen seed, without being sown. It is distinct from intentional cultivation and might seem harvestable without violating the prohibition — yet the text explicitly forbids even this. The LXX renders αὐτόματα ("the spontaneous/self-growing").

5. **"Undressed vine" / "Undressed vines" (vv. 5, 11):** Hebrew נְזִירִים, from נָזִיר ("separated, consecrated"). The vine that has not been pruned or tended is called a "Nazirite vine" — separated from cultivation, allowed to grow wild. LOGOS renders "undressed vine" following the agricultural tradition (ESV, NASB); the word's cognate relationship with Nazirite is noted but preserved implicitly. The LXX renders ἡγιασμένα ("consecrated/sanctified vines"), drawing the holiness language explicitly.

6. **"Seven weeks of years" (v. 8):** Hebrew שֶׁבַע שַׁבְּתֹת שָׁנִים. The word שַׁבָּת ("Sabbath") used for a year-unit, producing "Sabbath-years." Seven Sabbath-years (49 years) is the counting period for the Jubilee. LOGOS renders "weeks of years" following the standard formal-equivalence tradition and paralleling the use of שָׁבוּעַ ("week") in Dan 9:24–27. The LXX reads ἑπτὰ ἑβδομάδας ἐτῶν.

7. **"Loud trumpet" (v. 9):** Hebrew שׁוֹפַר תְּרוּעָה. The shofar (ram's horn) with a blast (תְּרוּעָה) of alarm/joy — the same combination that signals battle, covenant assemblies, and divine appearance (Exod 19:16; Num 10:5–6). LOGOS renders "loud trumpet" rather than "ram's horn" to preserve register and parallel the LXX σάλπιγγος φωνήν ("sound of a trumpet"), though the shofar is specifically a horn, not a metal trumpet. The choice is traditional in formal-equivalence versions.

8. **"Proclaim liberty throughout the land" (v. 10):** Hebrew וּקְרָאתֶם דְּרוֹר בָּאָרֶץ. The noun דְּרוֹר ("liberty, release") is a technical term with an Akkadian cognate andurārum, referring to the royal proclamation of debt-release in the ancient Near East. It appears also in Isa 61:1 and Jer 34:8, 15, 17. LOGOS renders "proclaim liberty" — the same phrase inscribed on the Liberty Bell (Philadelphia, 1751, quoting KJV). The LXX renders ἄφεσιν ("release, forgiveness").

9. **"Jubilee" (vv. 10, 11, 12, 13, etc.):** Hebrew יוֹבֵל, from the root related to יָבַל ("to flow, to carry along") or possibly from the ram's horn (יוֹבֵל = ram). The year is inaugurated by the shofar blast. LOGOS renders "Jubilee" throughout, the standard formal-equivalence rendering that has entered English as a loanword. The LXX phonetically transcribes ἰωβήλ or renders ἄφεσις depending on context.

10. **"Each of you shall return to his property" (v. 10):** Hebrew אִישׁ אֶל-אֲחֻזָּתוֹ. The noun אֲחֻזָּה ("possession, holding") is the technical term for inherited ancestral land in the land-theology of Leviticus and Numbers. It is distinct from purchased land (מִקְנֶה). At Jubilee, all alienated ancestral holdings revert to original families. The LXX renders τὴν κτῆσιν αὐτοῦ ("his property/possession").

11. **"Return to his clan" (v. 10):** Hebrew אֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ. The מִשְׁפָּחָה ("clan, family unit") is the intermediate social unit between the household (בֵּית אָב) and the tribe (שֵׁבֶט). The Jubilee restores not only land but social belonging — the person returns to the extended family network. LOGOS renders "clan" consistently for מִשְׁפָּחָה.

12. **"You shall not wrong one another" (vv. 14, 17):** Hebrew לֹא תוֹנוּ אִישׁ אֶת-עֲמִיתוֹ, from the root יָנָה ("to oppress, to wrong, to cheat"). The same verb appears twice: once in the commercial context of sale prices (v. 14) and once elevated to the moral principle with theological grounding — "you shall fear your God" (v. 17). The repetition creates an inclusio around the price-calculation legislation (vv. 14–16). The LXX uses ἀδικήσητε ("you shall not do injustice").

13. **"The land is mine" (v. 23):** Hebrew כִּי-לִי הָאָרֶץ. This three-word clause is the theological fulcrum of the entire chapter. Israel does not own the land; they hold it as tenant-farmers for the divine landlord. The permanent, inalienable character of land sale is impossible because no one can sell what they do not ultimately own. This principle grounds the entire Jubilee institution. The LXX renders ἐμὴ γάρ ἐστιν ἡ γῆ ("for the land is mine").

14. **"Strangers and sojourners with me" (v. 23):** Hebrew כִּי-גֵרִים וְתוֹשָׁבִים אַתֶּם עִמָּדִי. The two terms גֵּר ("stranger, sojourner") and תּוֹשָׁב ("resident alien, settler") appear together here applied to Israel itself — the very categories Israel uses for foreigners in its midst are now applied to Israel's own status before God. The phrase "with me" (עִמָּדִי) is striking: God's own dwelling in the land means Israel shares the land with its divine owner. The LXX renders παρεπίδημοι καὶ πάροικοι ("pilgrims and sojourners").

15. **"Redemption of the land" / "nearest redeemer" (vv. 24–26):** Hebrew גְּאֻלָּה ("redemption") and גֹּאֵל ("redeemer"). The root גאל ("to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer") carries obligations of family loyalty — the nearest male relative has both the right and duty to buy back alienated family land. The institution of the גֹּאֵל is central to Ruth and Jeremiah 32. LOGOS renders גֹּאֵל as "redeemer" or "nearest redeemer" rather than the longer phrase "kinsman-redeemer" to maintain lexical brevity while signaling the familial character in context. The LXX renders λυτρωτής ("redeemer/ransomer").

16. **"Becomes poor" (vv. 25, 35, 39, 47):** Hebrew יָמוּךְ אָחִיךָ, from מוּךְ ("to be brought low, to sink into poverty"). This verb appears four times in the chapter, each time opening a new case in the graduated scale of impoverishment: (1) selling land (v. 25); (2) needing personal support (v. 35); (3) selling oneself into indentured service to a fellow Israelite (v. 39); (4) selling oneself to a foreigner (v. 47). The structure is a descending scale of economic catastrophe, with corresponding provisions for each level. LOGOS renders "becomes poor" consistently for מוּךְ.

17. **"Brother" (vv. 25, 35, 36, 39, 47):** Hebrew אָחִיךָ ("your brother"). The repeated use of "brother" language for fellow Israelites in poverty is not incidental — it is the moral and social argument for the legislation. The impoverished person is not an abstract debtor but a covenant sibling. The term occurs seven times in the chapter (vv. 25, 35, 36, 39, 47, 48, 53). LOGOS preserves "brother" throughout rather than smoothing to "fellow Israelite" or "countryman."

18. **"As though he were a sojourner and a resident alien" (v. 35):** Hebrew כְּגֵר וְכַתּוֹשָׁב. A fallen Israelite brother is to be treated with the same provisions that protect non-Israelite resident aliens — a striking expansion of the humanitarian obligation. The analogy runs in reverse from v. 23: there Israel-as-nation is sojourner with God; here the individual poor brother is sojourner with the economically stable Israelite. The LXX renders ὡς προσήλυτος καὶ πάροικος.

19. **"Interest" and "profit" (vv. 36–37):** Hebrew נֶשֶׁךְ ("interest," lit. "a bite") and תַּרְבִּית ("increase, profit," from רָבָה "to multiply"). Both terms refer to charging more than was lent: נֶשֶׁךְ typically for money loans (the bite taken from the principal), תַּרְבִּית for commodity loans (grain returned with excess). LOGOS distinguishes them as "interest" and "profit" to preserve the MT's use of two distinct terms. The LXX renders τόκον ("usury/interest") and πλεονασμόν ("excess/surplus").

20. **"Make him serve as a slave" (v. 39):** Hebrew לֹא-תַעֲבֹד בּוֹ עֲבֹדַת עָבֶד, lit. "you shall not make him work the work of a slave." The construction stresses the nature of the service (עֲבֹדַת עָבֶד, "slave-labor") rather than mere slave-status. An indentured Israelite must be treated as a hired worker (שָׂכִיר) or sojourner (תּוֹשָׁב), not subjected to the demanding, permanent, chattel-type labor regime associated with foreign slavery. The LXX renders οὐ δουλεύσει σοι δουλείαν οἰκέτου ("shall not serve you as a household slave").

21. **"You shall not rule over him ruthlessly" (vv. 43, 46, 53):** Hebrew לֹא-תִרְדֶּה בוֹ בְּפָרֶךְ. The verb רָדָה means "to rule, to have dominion" — in non-negative contexts used of legitimate authority. The adverb בְּפָרֶךְ ("with harshness, with crushing force") qualifies the ruling as oppressive. The same combination appears in Exod 1:13–14 of Egypt's treatment of Israel — the Exodus is the controlling analogy for why Israel must not treat its own poor harshly. LOGOS renders "rule over him ruthlessly." The LXX reads οὐ κατατενεῖς αὐτόν ("you shall not oppress him").

22. **"They are my servants" / "For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants" (vv. 42, 55):** Hebrew כִּי-עֲבָדַי הֵם (v. 42) and כִּי-לִי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל עֲבָדִים (v. 55). The word עֶבֶד ("servant, slave") is used deliberately: because Israel belongs to God as his servants (עֲבָדִים), they cannot belong permanently to any human master. The logic is one of exclusive divine ownership. The same word used for human slavery is used for Israel's relationship to God — but as liberating rather than degrading. The chapter closes with this theological declaration echoing its opening premise: the land belongs to God (v. 23), and so do its people (v. 55). The LXX renders ἐμοὶ γὰρ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ οἰκέται ("for the sons of Israel are my household servants").

23. **"The nations that are around you" (v. 44):** Hebrew מֵאֵת הַגּוֹיִם אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבֹתֵיכֶם. The legislation permits acquisition of foreign slaves from surrounding nations while prohibiting permanent enslavement of Israelites. The distinction is grounded in covenant identity, not in ethnic superiority — Israelites are uniquely God's servants (vv. 42, 55). LOGOS does not soften or obscure this legal distinction, which is present in the text and must be rendered accurately. The LXX reads ἀπὸ τῶν ἐθνῶν τῶν κύκλῳ ὑμῶν.

24. **"Uncle / cousin" (v. 49):** Hebrew דֹּד ("uncle," paternal uncle) and בֶּן-דּוֹד ("son of an uncle," i.e., cousin). The redemption circle expands outward from closest to more distant relatives: brother (v. 48), uncle, cousin, "near relative from his clan" (v. 49), and finally the man himself (v. 49). This graduated list reflects the practical operation of kinsman-redeemer obligations in ancient Israelite society. The LXX renders πατράδελφος ("paternal uncle") and υἱὸς πατραδέλφου ("son of a paternal uncle").

25. **LXX Significant Variants:**
    - V. 10: LXX renders "Jubilee" as both ἄφεσις ("release") and ἰωβήλ (phonetic transliteration) in different verse sections of Vaticanus — the chapter is not entirely consistent in its rendering, reflecting possible editorial layering.
    - V. 30: LXX adds a plus after "walled city" specifying βεβαιωθήσεται ("it shall be confirmed/ratified") — making the permanence of the sale more emphatic than the MT.
    - V. 31: LXX renders כָּר ("villages without walls") as ἀγρός ("field") rather than a term for settlement — possibly conflating the village houses with field land.
    - V. 34: LXX omits the reference to the Levites' pastureland as "permanent possession" (אֲחֻזַּת עוֹלָם), shortening the verse and losing the emphasis on the perpetuity of the Levitical land grant.
    - V. 40: LXX renders תּוֹשָׁב ("resident alien/sojourner") as πάροικος ("sojourner"), consistent with its rendering elsewhere; but uniquely here pairs it with μισθωτός ("hired worker") in the same clause as the MT, preserving the MT's double status for the indentured Israelite.
    - V. 47: LXX renders "resident alien" as προσήλυτος ("proselyte") rather than the more neutral πάροικος ("sojourner"), continuing the later Jewish narrowing of גֵּר to religious convert rather than mere resident foreigner.

26. **Conjunctive "And" (throughout):** The Hebrew waw-consecutive is rendered "And" at the opening of verses and major clauses throughout, preserving the Hebrew paratactic narrative style in formal equivalence.
